title: FamilyRelationships

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_5 -- "FamilyRelationships".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package -- the parent main.tex in
% this folder provides the preamble.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_5
% title: FamilyRelationships
\def\rowref{def\_3\_5}
\def\rowtitle{FamilyRelationships}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_5}

\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
     We then define:
     \begin{enumerate}
         \item The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
         %\item[] \PF{maybe better for sets (???) :The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \lp \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)\rp \sm A.\]
         %    but then  if $v \tuh v \in E$ then $v \notin \Pa^G(\lC v\rC ) \neq \Pa^G(v) \ni v$ ... }
         \item The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
         %\item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
         \item The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
         \item The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } 
             v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
         \item[] The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.
         %\item The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $v$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ collider walk: } 
         %    v \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus w \in G \rC \sm \{v\}.\]
         %    Note: $\lp\Pa^G(v) \cup \Ch^G(v) \cup \Pa^G(\Ch^G(v))\rp \sm \{v\} \ins \MBl^G_d(v)$.
         %\item[] The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \MBl^G_d(v) \sm A.\]
         %\item The $\sigma$-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_\sigma(A):= \MBl^{G^\acy}_d(A), \]
         %    where $G^\acy$ is an acyclification of $G$, a notion defined below in Definition~\refrow{def_3_21}.
     \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. We then define:

  1. The set of parents of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
  2. The set of parents of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
  3. The set of children of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
  4. The set of children of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
  5. The set of siblings of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
  6. The set of ancestors of $v$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
  7. The set of ancestors of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
  8. The set of descendants of $v$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
  9. The set of descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
  10. The set of non-descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
  11. The strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
  12. The (union of) strongly connected components of $A$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
  13. The district of $v$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
  14. The district of $A$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- parents of a vertex v
lean.comments:
--
-- The set of *parents* of `v` in `G` is the set of vertices
-- `w Ōłł G` such that there is a *directed* edge `w Ō¤Č[G] v`
-- (equivalently `(w, v) Ōłł G.E`). This is the LN's `\Pa^G(v)`.
-- Bidirected edges are deliberately excluded here: a
-- bidirected edge `w Ō¤Ę v` makes `v` a *sibling* of `w` (see
-- the `Sib` operator below), never a parent.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 1, vertex variant):

  The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$:
  \[\Pa^G(v):=\{w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G\}.\]
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Directed-only.** The LN's `\tuh` is the directed-edge atom
--   (def_3_2), excluding `\huh` (bidirected). This matches the
--   def_3_3 split between "into" (arrowhead, includes `\huh`)
--   and "out of" (directed only, excludes `\huh`): parents are
--   read off "out of" edges from the parent's perspective.
--
-- * **`w Ōłł G` guard kept** -- see the module docstring. For
--   parents the guard is propositionally redundant via
--   `G.E_subset`, but kept for textual fidelity and uniformity
--   with the walk-based operators in the sibling files.
--
-- * **Parents can lie in `G.J`.** The LN allows directed edges
--   `j Ō¤Č v` with `j Ōłł J`, so input nodes can be parents of
--   output nodes. We do *not* restrict the parent set to `G.V`.
--   This is critical for chapters 4 -- 5 (CBN factorisation,
--   do-calculus): a CBN factorises `P(V | J)` as a product over
--   `Pa^G(v)`, and the parent set of an output `v` can include
--   input nodes (which is exactly how inputs enter the
--   factorisation).

/-- `Pa G v` -- the set of *parents* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w Ōłł G` with a directed edge `w Ō¤Č[G] v`. Matches the LN's
`\Pa^G(v)` (`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`, def 3.5
item 1). Parents can lie in either `G.J` or `G.V`. -/

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- parents of a set A
lean.comments:
--
-- The set of parents of a *set* `A` of vertices in `G` is the
-- union of the individual parent sets. The LN writes this as a
-- big-union over `v Ōłł A`; we use the standard `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, _`
-- (Mathlib `Set.iUnionŌéé`) idiom.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 1, set variant):

  The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$:
  \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **`Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Pa G v` (Mathlib's `Set.iUnionŌéé` shape), not
--   `Set.image (Pa G) A` or `{w | Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł Pa G v}`.**
--   `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, _` is the standard Mathlib idiom for "union
--   indexed over a set"; its membership lemma `Set.mem_iUnion`
--   (composed twice) unfolds to exactly the existential we want
--   in downstream proofs. `Set.image (Pa G)` would not
--   type-check (`Pa G : ╬▒ ŌåÆ Set ╬▒`, not `╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒`); the bare
--   set-builder would force every caller to do the
--   `simp [Set.mem_iUnion]` themselves.
--
-- * **The LN's commented-out `\PF{ŌĆ”}` alternative** (parents
--   of `A` minus `A` itself) is explicitly NOT what we
--   formalise -- the rendered text uses the plain bigunion. The
--   commented-out alternative motivates the LN's worry about
--   self-loops `v Ō¤Č v`; in our setup `v` is allowed to be its
--   own parent if `(v, v) Ōłł G.E`, and the parents of `{v}`
--   contain `v` in that case. Downstream rows that need to
--   exclude self-loops do so explicitly at the use site.

/-- `PaSet G A` -- the set of *parents* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`: the union of the parent sets of each `v Ōłł A`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Pa^G(A)`. -/
lean.statement:
def PaSet (G : CDMG ╬▒) (A : Set ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Pa G v
@[simp] theorem mem_PaSet {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒} {w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł PaSet G A Ōåö Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł Pa G v := by
  simp only [PaSet, Set.mem_iUnion, exists_prop]

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- children of a vertex v
lean.comments:
--
-- Symmetric mirror of `Pa`: the children of `v` are the vertices
-- `w Ōłł G` reached by a directed edge from `v`, i.e.
-- `v Ō¤Č[G] w`. Bidirected edges are again excluded; the
-- bidirected partners of `v` are its *siblings*, not children.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 2, vertex variant):

  The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$:
  \[\Ch^G(v):=\{w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G\}.\]
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Same shape as `Pa`, mirrored.** The argument order in the
--   edge swaps: `Pa G v` looks for `w Ō¤Č v` (edges INTO `v`),
--   `Ch G v` looks for `v Ō¤Č w` (edges OUT OF `v`). The LN's
--   "parents" and "children" are dual via the reversal of the
--   directed edge, and the def_3_3 vocabulary captures the same
--   duality (`EdgeOutOf G v w` Ōåö `tuh G v w` Ōåö `v Ō¤Č[G] w`).
--
-- * **Children are always in `G.V`.** Unlike parents, children
--   *cannot* be input nodes: `v Ō¤Č[G] w` means `(v, w) Ōłł G.E`,
--   and `G.E_subset : G.E ŌŖå (J Ōł¬ V) ├Ś╦ó V` forces the *target*
--   `w` to lie in `G.V`. So `Ch G v ŌŖå G.V`. This asymmetry --
--   "parents range over `J Ōł¬ V`, children over `V`" -- is
--   exactly the LN's encoding that "interventions point at
--   outputs, never the other way around".

/-- `Ch G v` -- the set of *children* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w Ōłł G` with a directed edge `v Ō¤Č[G] w`. Matches the LN's
`\Ch^G(v)`. Children are always output nodes (`w Ōłł G.V` follows
from `G.E_subset`); inputs cannot be children. -/

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- children of a set A
lean.comments:
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 2, set variant):

  The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$:
  \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- Identical to `PaSet`: `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Ch G v`. No further nuance.

/-- `ChSet G A` -- the set of *children* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Ch^G(A)`. -/
lean.statement:
def ChSet (G : CDMG ╬▒) (A : Set ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Ch G v
@[simp] theorem mem_ChSet {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒} {w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł ChSet G A Ōåö Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł Ch G v := by
  simp only [ChSet, Set.mem_iUnion, exists_prop]

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- siblings of a vertex v
lean.comments:
--
-- The set of *siblings* of `v` in `G` is the set of vertices
-- `w Ōłł G` with a *bidirected* edge `v Ō¤Ę[G] w` to `v`. This is
-- the LN's `\Sib^G(v)`. Symmetric in `v`/`w` thanks to
-- `G.L_symm` (def_3_1).
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 3):

  The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$:
  \[\Sib^G(v):=\{w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G\}.\]
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **No set variant.** Mirrors the LN strictly. The
--   commented-out `\item[] The set of children of A in G:` line
--   in the LN source (placed after `\Sib^G`) is *not* a
--   commented-out `\Sib^G(A)` -- it is a leftover comment
--   referencing children. The LN's intentional omission of
--   `\Sib^G(A)` stands.
--
-- * **Siblings are always in `G.V`.** Bidirected edges live in
--   `G.V ├Ś G.V` by `G.L_subset`, so `Sib G v ŌŖå G.V`. Like
--   children, inputs never participate as siblings.
--
-- * **Symmetric in `v` / `w`.** By `G.L_symm`, `v Ō¤Ę[G] w`
--   iff `w Ō¤Ę[G] v`, so `w Ōłł Sib G v Ōåö v Ōłł Sib G w` (with the
--   `w Ōłł G` and `v Ōłł G` guards adjusted). We do not state this
--   symmetry as a separate lemma here; it follows by `G.L_symm`
--   at the use site.

/-- `Sib G v` -- the set of *siblings* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w Ōłł G` with a bidirected edge `v Ō¤Ę[G] w`. Matches the LN's
`\Sib^G(v)`. The LN intentionally provides no set-input
counterpart, and neither do we. -/

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- district of a vertex v
lean.comments:
--
-- A vertex `w Ōłł G` is in the district of `v` if there is a
-- bidirected walk from `v` to `w`, i.e. a walk all of whose
-- edges are bidirected (`\huh`). Re-uses `Walk.IsBidirected`
-- from `WalkPredicates.lean`. As with `Anc`/`Desc`, the
-- existential phrasing matches the LN's "$\exists$ bidirected
-- walk" prose.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 8, vertex variant):

  The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$:
  \[\Dist^G(v) := \{w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: }
    v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \}.\]
  Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Walk direction `v ŌåÆ w`.** The LN writes the bidirected
--   walk as `v Ōåö v_1 Ōåö Ōŗ» Ōåö w`, starting at `v`. We mirror this:
--   `Walk G v w` is a walk *from* `v` to `w`. Because
--   bidirected edges are symmetric (`G.L_symm`), this choice is
--   propositionally symmetric -- but the *literal* walk
--   direction matches the LN.
--
-- * **All edges bidirected = `Walk.IsBidirected`.** The LN's
--   walk pattern `v Ōåö v_1 Ōåö Ōŗ» Ōåö w` is exactly the predicate
--   `Walk.IsBidirected` from `WalkPredicates.lean`: every step
--   is `bidir` (`\huh`). No directed-edge mixing allowed.
--
-- * **`w Ōłł G` guard load-bearing.** Same story as `Anc`/`Desc`
--   in `FamilyReachability.lean`: the trivial walk `Walk.nil v`
--   is vacuously bidirected, so without the guard every
--   `w : ╬▒` (even `w Ōłē G`) would land in `Dist G w`. The LN's
--   "Note: `v Ōłł Dist^G(v)`" relies on `v Ōłł G` from the
--   preamble; we encode that as an explicit hypothesis on
--   `self_mem_Dist`.
--
-- * **Bidirected walks model latent common causes.** The LN's
--   district is exactly the equivalence class of `v` under the
--   reflexive-transitive closure of the bidirected-edge
--   relation; downstream chapters use districts to identify
--   subsets of `V` that share latent confounders, which
--   matters for the ID algorithm (chapter 8) and for the FCI
--   algorithm's PAG completeness arguments (chapter 16).

/-- `Dist G v` -- the *district* of `v` in `G`: those `w Ōłł G` for
which there exists a *bidirected walk* (every step is `bidir`)
from `v` to `w`. Matches the LN's `\Dist^G(v)`. -/
lean.statement:
def Dist (G : CDMG ╬▒) (v : ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā ŽĆ : Walk G v w, ŽĆ.IsBidirected}
@[simp] theorem mem_Dist {G : CDMG ╬▒} {w v : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł Dist G v Ōåö w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā ŽĆ : Walk G v w, ŽĆ.IsBidirected :=
  Iff.rfl
theorem self_mem_Dist {G : CDMG ╬▒} {v : ╬▒} (hv : v Ōłł G) :
    v Ōłł Dist G v :=
  Ō¤©hv, Walk.nil v, by simpŌ¤®

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- district of a set A
lean.comments:
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 8, set variant):

  The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$:
  \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\]
  Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.
-/

/-- `DistSet G A` -- the *district* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Dist^G(A)`. -/
lean.statement:
def DistSet (G : CDMG ╬▒) (A : Set ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Dist G v
@[simp] theorem mem_DistSet {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒} {w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł DistSet G A Ōåö Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł Dist G v := by
  simp only [DistSet, Set.mem_iUnion, exists_prop]
theorem subset_Dist_set {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒}
    (hA : A ŌŖå G.J Ōł¬ G.V) : A ŌŖå DistSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_DistSet]
  exact Ō¤©v, hvA, self_mem_Dist (hA hvA)Ō¤®

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- ancestors of a vertex v
lean.comments:
--
-- A vertex `w Ōłł G` is an *ancestor* of `v` if there is a
-- directed walk from `w` to `v` in `G`. We re-use the
-- def_3_4 `Walk G w v` data type and the def_3_4
-- `Walk.IsDirected` predicate (from `WalkPredicates.lean`);
-- the existential "$\exists$ directed walk" matches the LN's
-- prose.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 4, vertex variant):

  The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$:
  \[\Anc^G(v):=\{w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: }
    w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G\}.\]
  Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **`Ōłā ŽĆ : Walk G w v, ŽĆ.IsDirected`, not a separate inductive
--   "ancestor" relation.** The plan-┬¦-equivalent reasoning from
--   `WalkPredicates.lean`'s `IsDirected` design block applies:
--   composing on top of the existing `Walk` data layer reuses
--   the full walk API (`length`, `support`, `append`,
--   `reverse`) for free, whereas a separate inductive would
--   require re-proving all of that. Plus, downstream rows
--   reason existentially on walks anyway (claim 3.2, def 3.6
--   acyclicity, ...).
--
-- * **Walk direction `w ŌåÆ v`.** The LN writes "directed walk:
--   `w \tuh Ōŗ» \tuh v`", i.e. the walk starts at `w` (the
--   candidate ancestor) and ends at `v` (the descendant). We
--   match exactly: `Walk G w v` is "a walk *from* `w` *to*
--   `v`", and the existential is over such walks. The opposite
--   direction is captured by `Desc^G` (next operator).
--
-- * **`w Ōłł G` guard is load-bearing here.** Without it, the
--   trivial walk `Walk.nil w : Walk G w w` -- which is
--   vacuously `IsDirected` (`Walk.isDirected_nil`) -- would
--   land every `w : ╬▒` in `Anc G w`, including vertices outside
--   `G.J Ōł¬ G.V`. With the guard, `Anc G v` is empty for `v Ōłē G`
--   in any sensible reading: for the trivial walk we'd need
--   `w = v` and `w Ōłł G`, both of which are constrained; for
--   longer walks the final step `ŌĆ” Ō¤Č[G] v` already forces
--   `v Ōłł G.V ŌŖå G` via `G.E_subset`. The LN's "Note: `v Ōłł
--   Anc^G(v)`" implicitly assumes the preamble's `v Ōłł V`, i.e.
--   `v Ōłł G`. We capture this as an explicit hypothesis on the
--   `self_mem_Anc` theorem below.

/-- `Anc G v` -- the set of *ancestors* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w Ōłł G` for which there exists a *directed walk* (in the
def_3_4 sense, i.e. `Walk G w v` plus `Walk.IsDirected`) from
`w` to `v`. Matches the LN's `\Anc^G(v)`. -/
lean.statement:
def Anc (G : CDMG ╬▒) (v : ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā ŽĆ : Walk G w v, ŽĆ.IsDirected}
@[simp] theorem mem_Anc {G : CDMG ╬▒} {w v : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł Anc G v Ōåö w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā ŽĆ : Walk G w v, ŽĆ.IsDirected :=
  Iff.rfl
theorem self_mem_Anc {G : CDMG ╬▒} {v : ╬▒} (hv : v Ōłł G) :
    v Ōłł Anc G v :=
  Ō¤©hv, Walk.nil v, by simpŌ¤®

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- ancestors of a set A
lean.comments:
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 4, set variant):

  The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$:
  \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\]
  Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- Same `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Anc G v` idiom as `PaSet` / `ChSet` (see
-- `FamilyDirect.lean`). The "Note: `A ŌŖå Anc^G(A)`" reflexivity
-- becomes the theorem `subset_Anc_set` below.

/-- `AncSet G A` -- the set of *ancestors* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Anc^G(A)`. -/
lean.statement:
def AncSet (G : CDMG ╬▒) (A : Set ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Anc G v
@[simp] theorem mem_AncSet {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒} {w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł AncSet G A Ōåö Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł Anc G v := by
  simp only [AncSet, Set.mem_iUnion, exists_prop]
theorem subset_Anc_set {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒}
    (hA : A ŌŖå G.J Ōł¬ G.V) : A ŌŖå AncSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_AncSet]
  exact Ō¤©v, hvA, self_mem_Anc (hA hvA)Ō¤®

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- descendants of a vertex v
lean.comments:
--
-- Mirror of `Anc`: walk direction is flipped to `v ŌåÆ w`.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 5, vertex variant):

  The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$:
  \[\Desc^G(v):=\{w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: }
    v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G\}.\]
  Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- Identical to `Anc` modulo direction reversal: the existential
-- walk is `Walk G v w` (from `v` to `w`) rather than
-- `Walk G w v`. All other shape considerations carry over
-- (`w Ōłł G` guard, `IsDirected` predicate from `WalkPredicates`,
-- existential phrasing).
--
-- We do *not* define `Desc G v` as `Anc (reverse G) v`
-- (reverse-graph trick) because we have not introduced a graph
-- reversal operation on `CDMG` -- and the LN does not use such a
-- trick at this stage; it works with the natural-direction walk
-- formulation throughout.

/-- `Desc G v` -- the set of *descendants* of the vertex `v` in
`G`: those `w Ōłł G` for which there exists a directed walk from
`v` to `w`. Matches the LN's `\Desc^G(v)`. -/
lean.statement:
def Desc (G : CDMG ╬▒) (v : ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā ŽĆ : Walk G v w, ŽĆ.IsDirected}
@[simp] theorem mem_Desc {G : CDMG ╬▒} {w v : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł Desc G v Ōåö w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā ŽĆ : Walk G v w, ŽĆ.IsDirected :=
  Iff.rfl
theorem self_mem_Desc {G : CDMG ╬▒} {v : ╬▒} (hv : v Ōłł G) :
    v Ōłł Desc G v :=
  Ō¤©hv, Walk.nil v, by simpŌ¤®

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- descendants of a set A
lean.comments:
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 5, set variant):

  The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
  \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\]
  Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
-/

/-- `DescSet G A` -- the set of *descendants* of the set `A` in
`G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Desc^G(A)`. -/
lean.statement:
def DescSet (G : CDMG ╬▒) (A : Set ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Desc G v
@[simp] theorem mem_DescSet {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒} {w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł DescSet G A Ōåö Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł Desc G v := by
  simp only [DescSet, Set.mem_iUnion, exists_prop]
theorem subset_Desc_set {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒}
    (hA : A ŌŖå G.J Ōł¬ G.V) : A ŌŖå DescSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_DescSet]
  exact Ō¤©v, hvA, self_mem_Desc (hA hvA)Ō¤®

lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- non-descendants of a set A
lean.comments:
--
-- `NonDesc^G(A) := (J Ōł¬ V) \ Desc^G(A)` ŌĆö every vertex of
-- `G` that is *not* a descendant of any vertex in `A`.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 6):

  The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
  \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **`(G.J Ōł¬ G.V) \ DescSet G A`, not `(Set.univ) \ DescSet G A`.**
--   The LN's ambient set is the graph's vertex set `J Ōł¬ V`, not
--   the ambient type `╬▒`. A vertex `w : ╬▒` outside `G.J Ōł¬ G.V` is
--   *not* a non-descendant; it simply isn't a vertex of `G` at
--   all. This is the same "ambient set is `J Ōł¬ V`" reading we
--   used for the `w Ōłł G` guard in the other operators -- a
--   non-descendant must in particular be in `G`.
--
-- * **Set-only operator (no vertex variant).** The LN provides
--   only `\NonDesc^G(A)`; introducing `NonDesc^G(v) := (J Ōł¬ V)
--   \setminus \Desc^G(v)` would be inventing a definition not
--   in the LN. The set version is the natural primitive --
--   downstream rows (do-calculus rules 2 -- 3, the ID
--   algorithm's exclusion criterion) always reason about
--   "non-descendants of *some set* `X`", never a single vertex.
--
-- * **Difference order: `(J Ōł¬ V) \ Desc, not Desc \ (J Ōł¬ V)`.**
--   Set difference is not symmetric -- the LN writes `(J \cup V)
--   \setminus \Desc^G(A)`, "ambient minus descendants", which
--   is what we encode.

/-- `NonDesc G A` -- the set of *non-descendants* of the set `A`
in `G`: vertices of `G` (i.e. members of `G.J Ōł¬ G.V`) that are
not descendants of any vertex in `A`. Matches the LN's
`\NonDesc^G(A)`. The LN provides no vertex-input variant. -/
lean.statement:
def NonDesc (G : CDMG ╬▒) (A : Set ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  (G.J Ōł¬ G.V) \ DescSet G A
@[simp] theorem mem_NonDesc {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒} {w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł NonDesc G A Ōåö w Ōłł G Ōł¦ w Ōłē DescSet G A := by
  simp [NonDesc, Set.mem_diff, CDMG.mem_iff]

lean.part: item 7: strongly connected component of a vertex
lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- strongly connected component of v
lean.comments:
--
-- Defined by intersection of `Anc^G(v)` and `Desc^G(v)` --
-- i.e. the set of `w Ōłł G` with a directed walk `w ŌåÆ Ōŗ» ŌåÆ v`
-- AND a directed walk `v ŌåÆ Ōŗ» ŌåÆ w`. By symmetry this carves
-- out the cyclic equivalence class of `v` under directed
-- reachability.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 7, vertex variant):

  The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$:
  \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\]
  Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **`Anc G v Ōł® Desc G v`, not a fresh walk-based primitive.**
--   The LN literally writes the intersection; we follow that
--   exactly. The `w Ōłł G` guard is inherited from `Anc` and
--   `Desc` (both have it baked in), so a vertex is in
--   `Sc G v` iff (i) it's in `G`, (ii) it has a directed walk
--   to `v`, and (iii) it has a directed walk from `v`.
--
-- * **Reflexivity `v Ōłł Sc^G(v)` composes from
--   `self_mem_Anc` and `self_mem_Desc`** -- this is the LN's
--   "Note:" line, encoded as the theorem `self_mem_Sc` below.

/-- `Sc G v` -- the *strongly connected component* of `v` in `G`:
the intersection of the ancestor and descendant sets of `v`.
Matches the LN's `\Sc^G(v)`. Equivalently, the set of `w Ōłł G`
that lie on a directed walk to `v` and on a directed walk from
`v`. -/
lean.statement:
def Sc (G : CDMG ╬▒) (v : ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  Anc G v Ōł® Desc G v
@[simp] theorem mem_Sc {G : CDMG ╬▒} {w v : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł Sc G v Ōåö w Ōłł Anc G v Ōł¦ w Ōłł Desc G v := Iff.rfl
theorem self_mem_Sc {G : CDMG ╬▒} {v : ╬▒} (hv : v Ōłł G) :
    v Ōłł Sc G v :=
  Ō¤©self_mem_Anc hv, self_mem_Desc hvŌ¤®

lean.part: item 7: strongly connected components of a set
lean.title: FamilyRelationships -- union of strongly connected components of a set A
lean.comments:
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.5,
item 7, set variant):

  The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$
  in $G$:
  \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\]
  Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- Same `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, _` idiom as the other set variants. The LN's
-- wording "(union of) strongly connected components" makes the
-- bigunion semantics explicit: `Sc^G(A)` is the union of each
-- `v Ōłł A`'s component, *not* the smallest strongly connected
-- component containing `A` (which would be a different
-- definition, and would not coincide with the union when `A`
-- spans more than one component).

/-- `ScSet G A` -- the union of *strongly connected components*
of vertices in `A`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Sc^G(A)`. -/
lean.statement:
def ScSet (G : CDMG ╬▒) (A : Set ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Sc G v
@[simp] theorem mem_ScSet {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒} {w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł ScSet G A Ōåö Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł Sc G v := by
  simp only [ScSet, Set.mem_iUnion, exists_prop]
theorem subset_Sc_set {G : CDMG ╬▒} {A : Set ╬▒}
    (hA : A ŌŖå G.J Ōł¬ G.V) : A ŌŖå ScSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_ScSet]
  exact Ō¤©v, hvA, self_mem_Sc (hA hvA)Ō¤®

lean_explanation: No Lean formalisation has been supplied for this entry yet: the `lean` array in the data row is empty. Once added, it would presumably introduce a bundle of definitions on a `CDMG` structure $G=(J,V,E,L)$ providing the family-relationship operators `Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib`, `Anc`, `Desc`, `NonDesc`, `Sc`, and `Dist`, each in both pointwise and set-indexed flavours. A faithful Mathlib-style rendering would likely express the pointwise versions as `Finset` or `Set` comprehensions over `V` filtered by the appropriate edge predicate, and lift them to subsets $A \subseteq J \cup V$ via `Finset.biUnion` or `Set.iUnion`. The walk-based notions (`Anc`, `Desc`, `Dist`) would call for a reflexive-transitive closure such as `Relation.ReflTransGen` on a directed-edge or bidirected-edge relation, capturing the inclusion $v \in \mathrm{Anc}^G(v)$ for free. `NonDesc` would be a straightforward `Set.diff` against the ambient node type, and `Sc` an `Inter` of `Anc` and `Desc`. Until the Lean block is provided, no specific Mathlib constructs or idioms can be cited from the source.
